toolbar.hint: Click any person to inspect · Yellow ring = You

environment.footer_note: Environmental and pathogen parameters shift individual CFS risk in real time. Pathogen severity scales base rates from influenza-like (~3% ME/CFS) to SARS-like (~27%). Societal stressors modulate outcomes independently.

detail.click_hint: Click any person in the simulation to see their individual profile, risk factors, and — if they develop ME/CFS — a brainstem disruption diagram.

generic.exposed: {name} was recently exposed. Symptoms may appear within days.
generic.infectious: {name} is in the acute phase and currently infectious.
generic.recovering: {name} is past the acute phase. Long-term outcome is still forming.
generic.long_covid: {name} has persistent post-viral symptoms. Many in this state recover over months; some do not.
generic.cfs: {name} has developed ME/CFS. Fatigue, PEM, and cognitive symptoms are limiting daily function.
generic.severe_cfs: {name} has severe ME/CFS. {name} is largely or fully bedbound.

viewpoint.healthy: You haven't been infected yet. Your risk factors are visible below — stay aware.
viewpoint.exposed: You've been exposed. You may not feel it yet, but the virus has entered your system.
viewpoint.infectious: You're in the acute phase. You're contagious. How your body handles this week matters a great deal.
viewpoint.recovering: The acute phase has passed. Your body is still processing. How this resolves will be determined over the coming months — autonomic involvement during your acute illness is the key signal.
viewpoint.recovered: You recovered fully. Your risk factors were present, but the outcome went in your favour.
viewpoint.long_covid: You have persistent post-viral symptoms. You're in the recovery window — many people in this state recover over 6–18 months. Some don't.
viewpoint.cfs: You've developed ME/CFS. This is your new reality. The brainstem disruption model below shows what's happening in your nervous system.
viewpoint.severe_cfs: You have severe ME/CFS. You are largely bedbound. The gap between your cognitive self and your physical capacity is profound.

sections.heading: What this simulates
sections.body: A viral outbreak moves through a community of 100 individuals. Each person's probability of developing ME/CFS post-infection is computed from a multi-factor risk model calibrated to published epidemiological research. Long COVID cases follow a decay curve toward recovery; ME/CFS cases represent the hard floor that persists.

sections.heading: Post-viral illness is not new
sections.body: Post-viral conditions appear after every major pandemic. Historical review across 150 years shows large numbers affected after the 1890 Russian flu, the 1918 Spanish flu, SARS (2003), H1N1 (2009), and COVID-19. COVID accelerated research into the underlying mechanisms substantially.

sections.heading: Prevalence across pathogens
list: COVID-19: WHO estimates 10–20% have symptoms beyond 3 months. Meta-analysis of 194 studies: 45% had at least one unresolved symptom at avg 126-day follow-up.
SARS (2003): 40% reported chronic fatigue at 4-year follow-up. 27% diagnosed with ME/CFS (Hong Kong cohort).
Influenza: 41% fatigue prevalence in survivors (meta-analysis). 20-year study: influenza patients had AHR 1.51 for developing CFS vs matched controls.
Ebola: 9–28% persistent fatigue. West Nile: 31%. Dengue: up to 82%.
Dubbo studies (EBV, Q fever, Ross River): 11% met ME/CFS criteria at 6 months — no significant difference across pathogens. Severity of acute infection was the main predictor.

sections.heading: The decay curve and the hard floor
sections.body: Long COVID prevalence in the U.S. declined from 18.9% (June 2022) to 11.0% (June 2023), then stabilised. The Chicago infectious mononucleosis study showed the same pattern: 13% met ME/CFS criteria at 6 months → 7% at 12 months → 4% at 24 months. Roughly 40–46% of remaining cases recover per 6-month window. The 4% floor represents those who do not recover — the ME/CFS reservoir. This simulation models both dynamics: Long COVID cases have a daily probability of recovery; ME/CFS cases do not.

sections.heading: Key predictors (cross-pathogen)
list: Severity of acute illness — the most replicated predictor across all studies.
Autonomic symptoms during acute phase — Chicago mono study: strongest single predictor of ME/CFS conversion, stronger than demographic factors.
Female sex — 2–3× elevated risk consistently across pathogens and studies.
Age 35–64 — peak risk for Long COVID phenotype (ONS data); older adults have elevated CFS risk from influenza.
Pre-existing conditions — autoimmune, metabolic, and psychiatric history all elevate risk.
Number of symptoms during acute phase.
Social support, sleep quality, economic stress — modulating factors throughout recovery.

sections.heading: Population-level burden
sections.body: Even a 2–5% chronic-illness conversion rate applied to pandemic-scale infections produces enormous absolute numbers. COVID infected billions globally; conservative estimates of persistent illness represent tens of millions added to the global disability burden — a slow-moving wave that follows the acute wave by 1–5 years and is much less visible in public discourse.

sections.heading: Brainstem disruption model
sections.body: For individuals who develop ME/CFS, the profile view shows a simplified diagram of the brainstem and autonomic nervous system. The model reflects the dorsal vagal complex hyperactivation hypothesis (Porges), HPA axis blunting, and vagal dysregulation. Key structures: Hypothalamus/HPA, Nucleus Tractus Solitarius (NTS), Dorsal Vagal Complex (DVC), Locus Coeruleus (LC), Vagus nerve.

sections.heading: References
sections.body: Wessely et al. 1995; Jason et al. 2006; Tomas & Newton 2018; Dubbo Infection Outcomes Study (Hickie et al.); Chicago mono cohort (Katz et al.); ONS Long COVID surveys 2022–2024; Hong Kong SARS follow-up (Lee et al.); Porges polyvagal theory; VanElzakker vagus nerve hypothesis; Naviaux metabolic model.

sections.heading: This is a model, not a prediction
sections.body: All values are illustrative. Individual outcomes are stochastic. The simulation is designed to make the systemic nature of post-viral illness tangible — not to provide clinical guidance.
